pivot_rationale.decision: Drop Flash from the CVE study battery. Pivot comparison to Flash Lite vs GPT-4o-mini vs Claude Haiku vs (Phase 3) a fine-tuned model.
evidence: Flash returned 503 UNAVAILABLE on 11/12 calls after 5 retries — endpoint is saturated and not benchmark-reliable.
The one Flash response that got through was truncated at max_tokens=256 mid-JSON, unparseable.
Flash Lite on the same run scored 9/12 vuln_class, 5/12 severity, 3/12 both — while self-reporting confidence 9-10 on every call.
Flash Lite's uniform high confidence on wrong severity answers is the exact `dangerous_rate` failure mode the pre-registration targets, visible in n=12.
pivot_rationale.why_more_interesting: Original hypothesis (Flash vs Flash Lite) was a capability gap. New hypothesis (Flash Lite vs better-calibrated models) is a calibration gap — directly aligned with the pre-registered `dangerous_rate` metric. Flash Lite is cheap + accurate on class + confidently wrong on severity; that is exactly where a calibration-aware router earns its keep.

amendments.reversal: Flash REINSTATED in the Phase 2 model roster.
amendments.reason: On review, the 503 UNAVAILABLE rate on 11/12 Flash calls was infrastructure timing (saturated endpoint during peak hours) and the one returned response was truncated by a fixable 256-token output budget — neither is a capability signal. Dropping Flash before measuring it would leave the research question 'does the larger Gemini model inherit Flash Lite's overconfidence or self-correct?' permanently unanswered.
actions_taken: GeminiFlash adapter: 3s minimum interval between calls on same instance (commit 25941d8)
Flash will be re-measured off-peak before Phase 2 commit #3
amendments.supersedes: The earlier 'dropped: [gemini-2.5-flash]' decision in this same pivot_rationale block is rescinded.
